papers.title: Improved catalytic efficiency of P450 OleP for converting lithocholic acid into murideoxycholic and ursodeoxycholic acids through semi-rational and rational design.
papers.abstract: Cytochrome P450 enzymes are promising for synthesizing therapeutically beneficial bile acids murideoxycholic acid (MDCA) and ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA) from lithocholic acid (LCA). However, the catalytic efficiency of P450 enzymes remains inadequate for large-scale production. Here, we address this limitation by engineering P450 OleP, achieving high activity for the efficient synthesis of MDCA and UDCA. Firstly, an improved high-throughput detection method for UDCA was developed to accelerate the screening process for mutants with improved UDCA synthesis capabilities. Next, the mutant S240A w
papers.summary: Researchers engineered the P450 enzyme OleP to more efficiently convert lithocholic acid into the therapeutic bile acids murideoxycholic acid and ursodeoxycholic acid. They developed a high-throughput screening method and identified the S240A mutant, which significantly improved catalytic efficiency for potential large-scale production.

papers.title: 3D-QSAR Modeling of the Anti-Inflammatory Activity of 28 Rare Biflavones.
papers.abstract: Inflammation underlies a wide range of chronic diseases. Current treatments, such as nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, are effective, but they often result in adverse side effects. This has driven the search for alternative anti-inflammatory agents with improved safety profiles. Biflavones have emerged as promising candidates, yet existing studies primarily focus on naturally occurring biflavones that are limited to those with C3'-C8″ linkages. In this study, we explore the anti-inflammatory potential of 28 biflavones featuring the underexplored C2'-C6″ linkage. Using LPS-induced RAW 264.7
papers.summary: This study investigates the anti-inflammatory potential of 28 rare biflavones with an underexplored C2'-C6″ linkage, using computational 3D-QSAR modeling. The findings identify key structural features that contribute to anti-inflammatory activity, offering a foundation for designing safer alternatives to current anti-inflammatory drugs.

papers.title: Design, Synthesis and Biological Effects Studies of Novel EGFR Inhibitors Targeting Wild-Type and Mutant EGFR (EGFR-L858R and EGFR-L858R/T790M).
papers.abstract: Lung cancer remains one of the most significant global health challenges. Although EGFR inhibitors are actively employed in treatment, there is an urgent need for novel and effective inhibitors. In this context, a series of new EGFR inhibitors targeting both wild-type and mutant EGFR were designed and synthesized. The anticancer potentials of the synthesized derivatives were evaluated on A549 (lung cancer) and NIH/3T3 (healthy fibroblast) cell lines using the MTT method. Biological activity results revealed that the derivatives with 3,4-dichloro (2i) and 2,4-dichloro (2j) substitutions on the
papers.summary: Researchers designed and synthesized new EGFR inhibitors that target both normal and mutant forms of the EGFR protein, which is important in lung cancer. The most promising compounds, particularly those with 3,4-dichloro and 2,4-dichloro substitutions, showed strong anti-cancer activity against lung cancer cells while being less toxic to healthy cells. This work provides potential new leads for treating lung cancers that have developed resistance to current EGFR-targeted therapies.

papers.title: Surface-specific film assembly of a Vibrio cholerae adhesin-derived peptide modulated by environmental salts.
papers.abstract: Underwater adhesion research increasingly draws on bioinspired systems to uncover the molecular mechanisms that enable strong interfacial binding in aqueous environments. The biofilm adhesin Bap1 from Vibrio cholerae contains a short peptide motif, SYWFFGWHTK (CP), which exhibits exceptional adhesive performance, surpassing mussel foot protein mfp5 under comparable conditions. Despite its promise, the roles of ionic environments and aggregation behavior in governing CP adhesion remain unclear. In this study, we investigate how ion identity influences CP aggregation, film formation, and interfa
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This study investigates how different environmental salts affect the surface-specific film assembly of a short peptide derived from the Vibrio cholerae biofilm adhesin Bap1. The peptide, SYWFFGWHTK, shows strong underwater adhesion that can be modulated by the ionic environment, providing insights into bioinspired adhesive design.

papers.title: Ultrafast excited-state proton transfer dynamics using linearized pair-density functional theory.
papers.abstract: Accurate simulation of excited-state bond-rearrangement dynamics remains a major challenge since photoinduced reactions can often involve significant changes in electronic structure along excited-state reaction pathways. Describing such processes requires an electronic structure method that provides balanced descriptions of all electronic states across the nuclear configuration space, while remaining computationally feasible for molecular dynamics. Linearized pair-density functional theory (L-PDFT) provides an efficient multireference framework for excited-state simulations by enabling an accu
papers.summary: This paper presents linearized pair-density functional theory (L-PDFT) as an efficient method for simulating excited-state bond-rearrangement dynamics, such as ultrafast proton transfer. The approach balances accuracy and computational feasibility, enabling molecular dynamics simulations of photoinduced reactions that involve significant electronic structure changes.

papers.title: Simulation of cell-size systems at long timescales with flexible protein structures.
papers.abstract: Protein behavior inside cells is dominated by the crowded nature of the intracellular environment. Progress in structure determination of proteins and protein complexes, based on advances in Artificial Intelligence, provides an opportunity for structure-based modeling of cellular phenomena. Such modeling at the atomic resolution has been advanced by the traditional simulation techniques, e.g. molecular dynamics. A recently developed docking-based approach implements Markov Chain Monte Carlo sampling of intermolecular energy landscapes, offering several orders of magnitude faster simulation pro
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This paper presents a new docking-based simulation method that uses Markov Chain Monte Carlo sampling to model protein behavior in crowded cellular environments, achieving speeds several orders of magnitude faster than traditional molecular dynamics. The approach leverages recent AI-driven advances in protein structure prediction to enable structure-based modeling of cellular phenomena at long timescales.

papers.title: HCGT-PL: a heterogeneous contrastive graph transformer unifying protein-ligand affinity prediction and structure-based virtual screening.
papers.abstract: Structure-based virtual screening and binding affinity prediction remain challenging due to solvation/entropy effects, protein flexibility, and induced fit. We present a Heterogeneous Contrastive Graph Transformer for Protein-Ligand (HCGT-PL) framework. Each complex is represented as a directed heterogeneous graph with multiple node and relation types; relation-specific multi-head attention enables message passing and aggregation. Unsupervised augmentations yield transferable interaction representations that are fine-tuned for affinity regression and virtual screening. Across diverse benchmark
papers.summary: This paper introduces HCGT-PL, a new deep learning framework that represents protein-ligand complexes as directed heterogeneous graphs to improve both binding affinity prediction and virtual screening. The model uses relation-specific attention and unsupervised contrastive learning to capture complex molecular interactions, outperforming existing methods on diverse benchmarks.

papers.title: Integrative immunoinformatics and structural modeling for the rational design of a multi-epitope vaccine candidate against human cytomegalovirus.
papers.abstract: Human cytomegalovirus (CMV) is a globally widespread pathogen associated with significant morbidity in immunocompromised individuals. Despite its clinical importance, no licensed vaccine is currently available. This study aimed to design a rational multi-epitope vaccine candidate targeting CMV using an integrative approach combining immunoinformatics and structural biology. Viral proteins were screened to identify epitopes with high affinity for B cells, cytotoxic T cells (CTLs), and helper T cells (HTLs) using the Immune Epitope Database (IEDB). Selected epitopes were filtered according to th
papers.summary: This study used computational methods to design a multi-epitope vaccine candidate against human cytomegalovirus (CMV), for which no licensed vaccine exists. By screening viral proteins for epitopes that stimulate B cells and T cells, the researchers constructed a vaccine construct and validated its stability and immunogenicity through structural modeling and molecular dynamics simulations.

papers.title: Decoding cytokine interactions for psoriasis therapy through computational repurposing of antihypertensive drugs.
papers.abstract: Psoriasis is a chronic, immune-mediated skin disorder characterized by excessive inflammation and the overexpression of pro-inflammatory cytokines, including IL-6, IL-1β, IL-17 A, and IFN-γ. Despite the availability of targeted therapies, current treatments often entail high costs, limited accessibility, and adverse side effects. Drug repurposing-utilizing approved medications for novel indications-offers a cost-effective strategy to accelerate the development of alternative therapies with established safety profiles. Twelve FDA-approved antihypertensive drugs-including ACE inhibitors, angiote
papers.summary: This study computationally repurposes FDA-approved antihypertensive drugs for psoriasis therapy by analyzing their interactions with key inflammatory cytokines. The findings suggest that certain antihypertensives may inhibit IL-6, IL-1β, IL-17A, and IFN-γ, offering a cost-effective alternative to current treatments with potentially fewer side effects.

papers.title: Discovery of indolylchalcone benzenesulfonamides as selective inhibitors of tumor-associated carbonic anhydrase IX and XII.
papers.abstract: Indole, characterized by its favorable bioavailability, unique structural attributes, and a broad pharmacological profile, represents a privileged scaffold in anticancer drug discovery. Chalcones constitute another important scaffold in medicinal chemistry and numerous chalcone derivatives have been reported to exhibit diverse biological activities, including anticancer and enzyme inhibitory effects. Several chalcone-based compounds have also been investigated as carbonic anhydrase inhibitors. Sulfonamides are a classical class of carbonic anhydrase (CA, EC 4.2.1.1) inhibitors with diverse the
papers.summary: This study reports the design and synthesis of novel indolylchalcone benzenesulfonamide compounds that selectively inhibit tumor-associated carbonic anhydrase IX and XII, which are potential targets for cancer therapy. The compounds show promise as selective anticancer agents by targeting these specific enzymes.

papers.title: Molecular dynamics investigation of interfacial energy and mechanical behavior in braid-reinforced hollow fiber membranes.
papers.abstract: This study presents a molecular dynamics investigation of braid-reinforced hollow fiber membranes to elucidate the interfacial and mechanical behaviors of polymeric composites composed of cellulose acetate (CA) and polyacrylonitrile (PAN). The analysis focuses on three representative configurations, homogeneous (CA/CA-II), semi-heterogeneous (PAN|CA/CA-I), and heterogeneous hybrid (CA/PAN-III), to evaluate their interfacial energies, adhesion mechanisms, and tensile responses. The calculated interfacial energies of - 6.32077, - 5.69262, and - 4.71113 mJ/m2 for CA/CA-II, PAN|CA/CA-I, and CA/PAN
papers.summary: This study uses molecular dynamics simulations to analyze the interfacial energy and mechanical behavior of braid-reinforced hollow fiber membranes made from cellulose acetate and polyacrylonitrile. The researchers found that homogeneous configurations have the highest interfacial energy, while heterogeneous hybrids show lower adhesion, providing insights for designing stronger composite membranes.

papers.title: Lenacapavir-induced lattice hyperstabilization is central to HIV-1 capsid failure at the nuclear pore complex and in the cytoplasm.
papers.abstract: Lenacapavir (LEN) is the first human immunodeficiency virus type 1 (HIV-1) capsid inhibitor approved for clinical use in humans. It inhibits multiple steps of the viral life cycle; however, the molecular details of the effect of LEN on capsid structure and the mechanistic steps of the inhibition are not understood. Recent studies show that intact cone-shaped capsids and capsids with LEN-induced breaks can dock at nuclear pore complexes (NPCs), but only intact capsids enter the nucleus. In this work, we combined large-scale coarse-grained molecular dynamics simulations and live-cell imaging to
papers.summary: This study reveals that the HIV-1 drug lenacapavir works by hyperstabilizing the viral capsid lattice, which prevents the capsid from properly interacting with nuclear pore complexes and blocks nuclear entry. Using simulations and live-cell imaging, the authors show that while drug-damaged capsids can still dock at the nuclear pore, only intact capsids can enter the nucleus, explaining lenacapavir's mechanism of action.

papers.title: Theoretical study on N(sp2)+-C(sp2)-H···O hydrogen bonds in the context of drug discovery.
papers.abstract: C-H···O hydrogen bonds are regarded as weak and overlooked in structure-based drug design. Here, we demonstrate through quantum-chemical calculations that N(sp2)⁺-C(sp2)-H···O hydrogen bonds involving pyridinium ions exhibit strong and directional interactions with carbonyl oxygen atoms. Density-functional-theory (DFT) and natural-bond-orbital (NBO) analyses indicated that cationization increases the positive charge on adjacent sp2 C-H protons, thereby enhancing their proton-donating ability. Fragment-based symmetry-adapted-perturbation-theory (F-SAPT) analyses revealed that these interactions
papers.summary: This study uses quantum-chemical calculations to show that N(sp2)⁺-C(sp2)-H···O hydrogen bonds involving pyridinium ions are strong and directional, contrary to the common view that C-H···O bonds are weak. The findings suggest these interactions could be leveraged in structure-based drug design to improve binding affinity.

papers.title: A structure-based epitope tagging approach identifies vulnerable sites on the malarial P36-P52 protein complex for antibody-mediated neutralization of Plasmodium sporozoites.
papers.abstract: Malaria is caused by apicomplexan parasites of the genus Plasmodium, which are transmitted through the bite of Anopheles mosquitoes that inject sporozoites (SPZs) into the skin. SPZs migrate to and infect the liver for an initial round of replication. SPZs and liver stages have long been considered as ideal targets for malaria vaccines. The main SPZ surface protein, the circumsporozoite protein (CSP), is the target of currently approved malaria vaccines and prophylactic antibody therapies. Studies in rodent malaria models have shown that anti-CSP antibodies exert their protective effect mainly
papers.summary: This study uses a structure-based epitope tagging approach to identify vulnerable sites on the P36-P52 protein complex of malaria sporozoites, which can be targeted by antibodies to neutralize the parasite before it infects the liver. The findings highlight new potential targets for malaria vaccine development beyond the currently targeted circumsporozoite protein.

papers.title: Structure of the Beat-Side complex reveals their interaction is required for neuromuscular system wiring.
papers.abstract: Nervous system assembly is guided by the actions of cell surface receptors. In Drosophila, members of the beaten path (Beat) and sidestep (Side) protein families have been described as axon guidance receptor-cue pairs, in addition to roles in specifying synaptic connectivity in the optic lobe. To understand the molecular basis and specificity of Beat-Side interactions, we report here the structure of a Beat-Side complex, Beat-Vc bound to Side-VI. The structure shows a binding topology similar to other immunoglobulin superfamily receptors, especially PD-1-PD-L1, nectins, SynCAMs, Dprs, and DIPs
papers.summary: This study reports the first structure of a Beat-Side protein complex (Beat-Vc bound to Side-VI), revealing a binding topology similar to PD-1-PD-L1 and other immunoglobulin superfamily receptors. The findings demonstrate that Beat-Side interactions are essential for proper neuromuscular system wiring in Drosophila, providing molecular insight into axon guidance and synaptic connectivity.

papers.title: Design and computational evaluation of E-stilbene-bearing 1,3,4-oxadiazole derivatives as potential tyrosinase inhibitors using DFT, molecular docking and ADMET studies.
papers.abstract: A series of E-stilbene-bearing 1,3,4-oxadiazole derivatives (4a-b and 5a-f) was synthesized via the Mizoroki-Heck reaction and evaluated as inhibitors of tyrosinase, a vital enzyme involved in melanin biosynthesis. The synthesized compounds were structurally confirmed through NMR spectroscopy (1H NMR, 13C NMR), infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and mass spectrometry. Nearly all derivatives exhibited inhibitory effects against tyrosinase with IC50 values ranging from 0.32 ± 0.03 µM to 6.28 ± 0.05 µM. Among them, compounds 5d and 5b illustrated the most significant inhibitory activity, with IC50 va
papers.summary: This study designed and tested new chemical compounds that inhibit tyrosinase, an enzyme involved in melanin production, with some showing strong activity at low concentrations. The most promising compounds were identified through computational modeling and lab experiments, suggesting potential applications in skin-lightening or anti-browning products.

papers.title: ANGPTL4 contributes to osimertinib resistance through NDRG1-associated extracellular matrix remodeling in EGFR-mutant NSCLC organoid models.
papers.abstract: As a first-line treatment for EGFR-mutated non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), acquired resistance to osimertinib has become a major clinical challenge. Traditional two-dimensional cell culture models have significant limitations in simulating the tumor microenvironment, making it difficult to fully elucidate complex resistance mechanisms. In recent years, three-dimensional organoid models have emerged as a crucial platform for studying resistance mechanisms due to their superior ability to preserve tumor tissue architecture and heterogeneity. Angiopoietin-like protein 4 (ANGPTL4), a secreted
papers.summary: This study uses 3D organoid models of EGFR-mutant non-small cell lung cancer to show that the secreted protein ANGPTL4 drives resistance to the drug osimertinib by remodeling the extracellular matrix via NDRG1. The findings highlight a new resistance mechanism and demonstrate the value of organoid models for studying tumor microenvironment interactions.

papers.title: The influence of ligands on AlphaFold3 prediction of cryptic pockets.
papers.abstract: Cryptic pockets-binding sites that are formed or exposed upon a conformational change-represent an important class of potentially druggable binding sites. Reliably predicting cryptic pockets capable of binding ligands, however, remains a challenge. Herein we examine the use of AlphaFold 3 (AF3) for generating realistic conformational ensembles that include known cryptic pockets. We find that AF3 is generally able to reproduce the scale of conformational change required for cryptic site formation. When given a cryptic-site ligand for the protein, AF3 predominantly predicts conformations compete
papers.summary: This study evaluates AlphaFold3's ability to predict cryptic pockets, which are hidden binding sites that become accessible only after a protein changes shape. The researchers found that AlphaFold3 can generally reproduce the large-scale conformational changes needed for these pockets to form, and when provided with a ligand that binds to a cryptic site, it predominantly predicts the open, ligand-bound conformation. This suggests AlphaFold3 could be a useful tool for identifying druggable cryptic pockets, though the work is an incremental validation of an existing method.

papers.title: Ketodarolutamide may interact with the receptor-binding domain of the SARS-CoV-2 spike glycoprotein: the importance of halogenated benzonitrile.
papers.abstract: Emerging pathogens such as SARS-CoV-2 are always the cause of extremely high morbidity and mortality. Despite a shared history with humanity spanning more than half a decade, COVID-19 treatment is still challenging, especially in people with comorbidities. The RBD of the S-glycoprotein is a promising target because it plays a key role in the tropism of SARS-CoV-2. Using AutoDock Vina together with a bash script FDA-approved compounds were virtually screened for their binding affinity to the RBD pocket, which was identified in our previous work. Nine promising compounds were selected based on t
papers.summary: This study used computational docking to screen FDA-approved drugs for binding to the SARS-CoV-2 spike protein's receptor-binding domain (RBD), identifying ketodarolutamide as a promising candidate. The compound's halogenated benzonitrile group appears important for binding, suggesting a potential repurposing opportunity for COVID-19 treatment.

papers.title: Therapeutic potential of Citrus medica L. (cv. 'Liscia' and cv. 'Rugosa') phytocompounds targeting biofilm formation, quorum sensing, and antioxidant defense mechanisms.
papers.abstract: Microbial biofilm, quorum sensing (QS)-mediated virulence and oxidative stress-induced inflammation are interrelated pathological processes that play a crucial role in persistent infections and chronic illnesses. Due to the multifactorially of such processes, isolated target-based approaches to therapy usually do not provide long-term effectiveness. A combined network pharmacology and multi-level computational approach was used in order to clarify the molecular mechanisms underlying the antibiofilm, anti-quorum sensing (anti-QS), and antioxidant activities of thirty-three bioactive compounds f
papers.summary: This study uses network pharmacology and computational modeling to investigate how bioactive compounds from two varieties of Citrus medica (citron) might simultaneously target biofilm formation, quorum sensing, and antioxidant defense mechanisms. The findings suggest that these phytocompounds could serve as multi-target therapeutic agents for persistent infections and chronic inflammatory diseases.

papers.title: Pangenome-guided immunoinformatics design and in silico characterization of a multi-epitope vaccine candidate against Acinetobacter baumannii with nanoparticle assembly potential.
papers.abstract: Acinetobacter baumannii is a critical multidrug-resistant pathogen causing severe healthcare infections with high mortality, yet no licensed vaccine exists. This study aims to identify universally conserved surface antigens through pangenome analysis, predict immunogenic epitopes using integrated machine learning, and computationally design and in silico characterize a self-assembling nanoparticle vaccine with dual adjuvants. A computational framework integrating pangenome analysis of 712 complete genomes, epitope prediction, and structural vaccinology was employed to design a multi-epitope na
papers.summary: Researchers used pangenome analysis of over 700 genomes to identify conserved surface antigens in the drug-resistant pathogen Acinetobacter baumannii, then designed a multi-epitope vaccine candidate using machine learning and structural vaccinology. The vaccine incorporates self-assembling nanoparticle technology and dual adjuvants, showing promising immunogenic potential in computational simulations. This study provides a computational blueprint for developing a vaccine against a critical pathogen where none currently exists.

papers.title: Network pharmacology and in silico analysis of Azadirachta indica phytoconstituents reveal potential hepatoprotective targets and mechanisms.
papers.abstract: Network pharmacology and bioinformatics approaches may provide valuable insights into pharmacological effects by enabling a system-level understanding of how drugs interact with biological networks rather than single targets. The study aimed to elucidate the therapeutic mechanisms of Azadirachta indica leaf extract (AILE) in hepatotoxicity and hepato-injury (HT/HI) through the identification of pathways and molecular targets via network pharmacology. Phytochemical profiling of AILE was performed by Gas Chromatography-Mass Spectrometry (GC-MS). SMILES structures of identified phytochemicals wer
papers.summary: This study uses network pharmacology and bioinformatics to identify how compounds from neem leaf extract may protect the liver from injury. By analyzing chemical profiles and simulating interactions with biological networks, the authors pinpoint potential molecular targets and pathways involved in hepatoprotection.

papers.title: A comprehensive dataset of 32 million pentapeptide structures for high-throughput virtual screening.
papers.abstract: Small peptides are widely used as binders, modulators, and structural motifs, but their conformational flexibility complicates structure-based analysis and high-throughput screening. We present an open dataset of three-dimensional structures for the complete space of canonical amino-acid pentapeptides: 3,200,000 unique sequences with up to 10 conformers per sequence, for a total of 32,000,000 peptide conformers. Structures were generated directly from sequence using an automated workflow built on UCSF ChimeraX for model construction, Reduce for hydrogen placement, and RDKit for conformer gener
papers.summary: This paper presents an open dataset of 32 million 3D structures covering all possible pentapeptide sequences, generated using an automated computational workflow. This resource enables high-throughput virtual screening and structure-based analysis of small peptides without requiring experimental structure determination.

papers.title: A systems herbal-to-molecule and transcriptomic strategy identifies emodin as an ESR1-targeting phytochemical driving thrombopoiesis.
papers.abstract: Radiation-induced thrombocytopenia (RIT) is a major dose-limiting toxicity of radiotherapy, for which effective pharmacological interventions remain limited. Rhei Radix et Rhizoma (RR) is widely used in traditional medicine, yet its thrombopoietic activity and molecular basis have not been systematically defined. Here, we investigated the effects of RR on thrombopoiesis using an integrated herbal-to-molecule and transcriptomic strategy. RR extract and representative phytochemicals were screened for megakaryocyte (MK) differentiation in Meg-01, K562, and murine haematopoietic stem and progenito
papers.summary: This study uses an integrated herbal-to-molecule and transcriptomic approach to identify emodin, a compound from the herb Rhei Radix et Rhizoma, as a promoter of platelet production by targeting the ESR1 receptor. The findings suggest a potential new treatment for radiation-induced thrombocytopenia, a common side effect of radiotherapy.

papers.title: A population-based retrospective machine learning study of COVID-19 severity using integrated clinical and viral genomic data in Jiangsu Province, China.
papers.abstract: As coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) has transitioned into an endemic phase characterized by sustained transmission and widespread hybrid immunity, understanding region-specific determinants of severe disease remains important for real-world risk stratification and public health planning.
papers.summary: This study used machine learning to analyze clinical and viral genomic data from COVID-19 patients in Jiangsu Province, China, identifying key factors associated with severe disease. The findings highlight region-specific risk determinants that can aid in real-world patient stratification and public health planning during the endemic phase.

papers.title: Molecular dynamics simulation of the atropine drug encapsulation inside protein origami icosahedral nanocages in physiological environment.
papers.abstract: Atomic nanocages are innovative nanostructures with significant potential in drug delivery systems. Their unique properties enable effective encapsulation and controlled release of therapeutic agents, making them a focal point in modern biomedical applications. Here, we used molecular dynamics (MD) approach to describe these atomic samples in drug delivery process of the target drug for clinical applications. The various atomic systems in our simulations consist of 8ED3 (S1) and 8XB8 (S2) nanocages and atropine drug. The MD simulations done in two main phases. Firstly, the equilibrium phase of
papers.summary: This study uses molecular dynamics simulations to investigate how the drug atropine can be encapsulated inside protein origami icosahedral nanocages for potential drug delivery applications. The simulations model the behavior of these nanocages in a physiological environment, providing insights into their stability and drug-loading capacity.

papers.title: Identification of phillyrin as a PPAR-γ ligand through network pharmacology and molecular modeling.
papers.abstract: Insulin resistance is a core pathological hallmark of metabolic syndromes like type 2 diabetes mellitus. Peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor gamma (PPAR-γ) is a classical therapeutic target for improving insulin sensitivity, yet the clinical utility of its synthetic agonists faces diverse adverse reactions. We previously showed that phillyrin, an important component of Forsythia suspensa, could improve insulin resistance in obesity. However, its direct molecular targets remain not fully understood. Herein, we adopted a combined in silico and experimental approach to determine whether ph
papers.summary: This study identifies phillyrin, a compound from Forsythia suspensa, as a direct ligand of PPAR-γ, a key target for improving insulin sensitivity. Using network pharmacology and molecular modeling, the authors demonstrate that phillyrin binds to PPAR-γ, potentially explaining its ability to improve insulin resistance in obesity. The findings suggest phillyrin could be a safer alternative to synthetic PPAR-γ agonists for treating type 2 diabetes.

papers.title: Time-resolved Coulomb explosion imaging of a photochemical ring opening reaction.
papers.abstract: Photoinduced ring-opening of cyclic organic molecules is fundamental to many photochemical processes, from vitamin D biosynthesis to molecular optical switching. Despite advances in several ultrafast techniques, direct and unambiguous imaging of the ultrafast nuclear motion is still a major challenge. As a consequence, reaction mechanisms remain controversial even for extensively studied prototypical systems. Here, we show that time-resolved Coulomb explosion imaging, together with molecular dynamics simulation, can be used to identify and map the ring-opening reaction of gas-phase furan follo
papers.summary: This study demonstrates that time-resolved Coulomb explosion imaging combined with molecular dynamics simulations can directly visualize the ultrafast nuclear motion during the photoinduced ring-opening of furan, a prototypical cyclic organic molecule. This approach resolves long-standing controversies about reaction mechanisms in photochemical ring-opening processes.

papers.title: Lipid packing contributes to the confinement of caveolae to the plasma membrane.
papers.abstract: Lipid packing is a fundamental characteristic of bilayer membranes. Yet, we lack detailed mechanistic understanding of how lipid packing directly affects membrane-associated cellular processes. Here, we address this by focusing on caveolae, small Ω-shaped invaginations of the plasma membrane, which serve as key regulators of cellular lipid sorting and mechano-responses. In addition to caveolae coat proteins, the lipid membrane is a core component of caveolae that critically impacts their biogenesis, morphology, and stability. We show that the small compound Dyngo-4a adsorbs and inserts into th
papers.summary: This study reveals that lipid packing, a fundamental property of cell membranes, plays a critical role in confining caveolae (small membrane invaginations) to the plasma membrane. The authors show that the compound Dyngo-4a inserts into the membrane and disrupts this confinement by altering lipid packing, providing mechanistic insight into how membrane lipid organization directly influences caveolae function.

papers.title: ScrambleBench: a workflow for comparative assessment of structure-based de novo generative models.
papers.abstract: Generative artificial intelligence (AI) has rapidly advanced over the past decade in the field of drug discovery, particularly for the de novo design of small molecules based on target protein structures. While generative AI has the potential to complement traditional structure-based drug design (SBDD) to discover novel hits, their performance is often assessed using non-standardized evaluation criteria. As the number of generative AI models continue to grow, it becomes increasingly important to determine whether these tools are sufficiently robust and reliable for integration into medicinal c
papers.summary: This paper introduces ScrambleBench, a standardized workflow for evaluating structure-based de novo generative models in drug discovery. The tool addresses the need for consistent and comparable assessment of generative AI models, which currently lack uniform evaluation criteria, to determine their robustness for medicinal chemistry integration.

papers.title: Metastable multimeric G-quadruplex 2'FY-RNA aptamers that selectively bind pyoverdines.
papers.abstract: Two 2'FY-RNA aptamers with distinct sequences were selected for specific binding to pyoverdine-Pf5 (PVD-Pf5), increasing chromophore fluorescence upon binding. They also recognized the peptide portion of pyoverdines, as shown by their differential specificity for related variants. Computational analysis and experimental data (NMM binding, CD spectra) identified G-quadruplex structures that were thermally metastable but reformed in the presence of PVD-Pf5. Further structural studies mainly with one aptamer revealed imino proton peaks in 1D H-NMR and pressure stability up to 2 kbar. Electrophore
papers.summary: This study reports the selection of two 2'FY-RNA aptamers that specifically bind to the bacterial siderophore pyoverdine-Pf5, increasing its fluorescence. The aptamers form metastable G-quadruplex structures that are stabilized by target binding, and they recognize both the chromophore and peptide portions of pyoverdines. This work provides a new class of RNA-based sensors for bacterial detection and siderophore research.

papers.title: A molecular dynamics study on anticorrosion properties of graphene/epoxy nanocomposites in NaCl medium.
papers.abstract: This work aims evaluate the role of graphene derivatives in epoxy coatings. We proposed new models composed of iron, graphene derivative/epoxy composites and a corrosion medium (NaCl), and evaluated the effects of the graphene derivatives in the epoxy coatings on the corrosion of iron by using Molecular Dynamics (MD) and Density Functional Theory (DFT). We used graphene derivatives such as pristine graphene (PG), graphene oxide (GO), reduced graphene oxide (RGO), and functionalized graphene oxide (FGO, where F represents two forms of nitrogen-based groups, which are amine -NH2 and amide -C([do
papers.summary: This study uses molecular dynamics and density functional theory to model how different graphene derivatives (pristine graphene, graphene oxide, reduced graphene oxide, and functionalized graphene oxide) in epoxy coatings protect iron from corrosion in a sodium chloride environment. The findings provide insights into the anticorrosion mechanisms of graphene/epoxy nanocomposites, which could inform the design of better protective coatings.

papers.title: Computationally optimized molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor based on biomass-derived biochar for paclobutrazol analysis in Radix Angelicae Sinensis.
papers.abstract: Paclobutrazol (PBZ) is widely used in agriculture, but its residues in medicinal herbs may compromise product safety and quality. In this work, a molecularly imprinted electrochemical sensor was developed for the determination of PBZ in Radix Angelicae Sinensis. The sensor integrates Angelica stalk-derived biochar as a sustainable porous carbon substrate with a molecularly imprinted layer rationally designed through a combined computational approach. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations (Dmol3 module) were employed to screen the optimal functional monomer (o-phenylenediamine), and Forc
papers.summary: This study developed a novel electrochemical sensor for detecting the pesticide paclobutrazol in a medicinal herb, using a sustainable biochar derived from the herb's own stalks and a computationally designed molecular imprinting layer. The sensor achieved high sensitivity and selectivity, offering a promising tool for ensuring the safety and quality of herbal products.

papers.title: Structural and biochemical insights into the antibiofilm activity of cationic lipoamino acid ester against methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus.
papers.abstract: Three-dimensional structures, primarily derived from X-ray crystallography, are crucial for understanding the solid-state properties of active pharmaceuticals and for enabling structure-based drug design. Herein, we report the first single crystal X-ray structure of cationic lipoaminoacid ester [(2-((N-(2-methoxy-2-oxoethyl)tetradecanamido)methyl)-1-methylpyridin-1-ium iodide, QPyN14Ge]. QPyN14Ge crystallized in the triclinic space group P1̄. QPyN14Ge has a highly ordered and coherent packing arrangement due to tail-to-tail interdigitation of hydrophobic layers, stabilization by planar trans-a
papers.summary: This study reports the first single-crystal X-ray structure of a cationic lipoamino acid ester (QPyN14Ge) with antibiofilm activity against methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus. The structure reveals a highly ordered packing arrangement with tail-to-tail interdigitation of hydrophobic layers, providing insights into its solid-state properties and potential for structure-based drug design against biofilm-associated infections.

papers.title: Engineering intrinsically radiolabeled nanoparticles for imaging and therapy: a decade of design strategies and insights.
papers.abstract: The convergence of nanotechnology and nuclear medicine has opened new possibilities for cancer imaging and therapy, but conventional chelator-based radiolabeling approaches often suffer from limited stability and can alter the inherent properties of nanomaterials. Intrinsic radiolabeling has emerged as a promising alternative, enabling the direct incorporation of radionuclides within the nanoparticle matrix through mechanisms such as lattice doping, isotopic substitution, coordination to structural sites, or entrapment during nanoparticle formation. In this approach, the radionuclide becomes a
papers.summary: This review summarizes a decade of design strategies for intrinsically radiolabeled nanoparticles, which incorporate radionuclides directly into the nanoparticle matrix to improve stability and preserve nanomaterial properties for cancer imaging and therapy. The authors discuss mechanisms like lattice doping and isotopic substitution, highlighting how this approach overcomes limitations of conventional chelator-based methods.

papers.title: N-MIX: an in silico framework for predicting ADAM10-mediated substrate cleavage sites through structural and spatial analysis of protease-substrate interactions.
papers.abstract: ADAM10 is a membrane-anchored metalloprotease that mediates ectodomain shedding of diverse substrates implicated in cancer, inflammation, and neurodegeneration. Despite its biological significance, ADAM10 substrate specificity remains poorly understood, as conventional sequence-based analyses have proven insufficient to identify conserved cleavage motifs. Through three-dimensional structural analysis of ADAM10-substrate complexes predicted by AlphaFold2, we identified four key determinants governing ADAM10-mediated proteolytic cleavage: (1) conserved hydrogen bond interactions at the enzyme-su
papers.summary: This study presents N-MIX, a computational framework that uses AlphaFold2-predicted 3D structures of ADAM10-substrate complexes to identify key structural determinants of substrate cleavage, overcoming the limitations of traditional sequence-based motif analysis. By analyzing hydrogen bond interactions and spatial features, the framework accurately predicts ADAM10-mediated cleavage sites, offering a new approach for studying protease specificity in cancer, inflammation, and neurodegeneration.

papers.title: MLAC: MicroED-assisted ligand structure analysis in complexes and its application to hERG-ligand complexes.
papers.abstract: Accurate determination of ligand structures in protein-ligand complexes is essential for elucidating molecular recognition mechanisms and advancing structure-based drug discovery. Cryogenic electron microscopy (cryo-EM) has emerged as a powerful technique for determining macromolecular structures; however, reliable identification of small-molecule ligands from cryo-EM maps remains challenging, particularly in the absence of accurate initial ligand models. Here, we present MLAC (MicroED-assisted Ligand structure Analysis in Complexes), an integrative framework that combines microcrystal electro
papers.summary: MLAC is a new integrative framework that combines microcrystal electron diffraction (MicroED) with cryo-EM to accurately determine small-molecule ligand structures in protein-ligand complexes, addressing a key challenge in structure-based drug discovery. The method was successfully applied to hERG-ligand complexes, demonstrating its utility for reliable ligand identification when initial models are lacking.

papers.title: Invited review: Manufacturing Whey Protein Colloidal Particles via Liquid Antisolvent Precipitation Method: Particle Formation Mechanism and Ingredient Functionality Aspects.
papers.abstract: The liquid antisolvent (LAS) precipitation, also referred to as desolvation, has emerged as a promising strategy to manufacturing whey protein particles (WPP) with tunable structures and functionalities. In contrast with conventional heat-induced protein aggregation, protein aggregation induced by LAS processing is driven by protein supersaturation generated by a rapid reduction in solvent quality upon mixing with an antisolvent under ambient conditions. This review consolidates current knowledge on the mechanisms of LAS induced protein-protein aggregation, LAS processing variables, physicoche
papers.summary: This review examines the liquid antisolvent (LAS) precipitation method for manufacturing whey protein particles, which offers tunable structures and functionalities compared to conventional heat-induced aggregation. The method works by rapidly reducing solvent quality to create protein supersaturation and aggregation under ambient conditions.

papers.title: TEDLH: Domain HMMs for sensitive detection of remote homologues.
papers.abstract: The Encyclopedia of Domains (TED) provides domain annotations for proteins in the AlphaFold Protein Structure Database (AFDB) using a consensus of three state-of-the-art structure-based methods. We used these annotations to construct profile Hidden Markov models (HMMs), collectively forming the TED Library of HMMs (TEDLH). TEDLH enables sensitive sequence and profile searches, supporting systematic exploration of protein domain families and their evolutionary relationships.
papers.summary: The authors created a library of profile Hidden Markov models (TEDLH) based on domain annotations from the Encyclopedia of Domains (TED), which uses consensus structural methods on AlphaFold protein structures. TEDLH enables sensitive detection of remote protein homologues through sequence and profile searches, aiding the study of domain families and evolution.

papers.title: Molecular modeling of 7-propanamide benzoxaboroles as CPSF3 inhibitors through docking-based 3D-QSAR and molecular dynamics simulations.
papers.abstract: Benzoxaboroles are boron-based heterocycles with broad therapeutic potential, including two FDA-approved drugs, tavaborole and crisaborole. It was reported that the discovery of 7-propanamide benzoxaboroles as novel potent anti-cancer agents, with compound 44 inhibiting cleavage and polyadenylation specific factor 3 (CPSF3), a key mRNA processing enzyme. By blocking CPSF3's RNA-binding function, compound 44 disrupts pre-mRNA cleavage and induces cancer cell death. In our current study, we systematically investigated the structure-activity relationships (SARs) of a series of 7-propanamide benzo
papers.summary: This study uses computational methods (docking-based 3D-QSAR and molecular dynamics simulations) to explore how 7-propanamide benzoxaboroles inhibit the mRNA processing enzyme CPSF3, a potential anti-cancer target. The work provides structural insights into the binding of these compounds, which could aid in designing more potent CPSF3 inhibitors.

papers.title: Structure-based simulations of the full Flock House virus capsid reveal pathways and energetics of an infection-critical peptide externalization event.
papers.abstract: Viral capsids are metastable assemblies whose large-scale dynamics enable critical steps in infection, including the exposure of internal protein segments that interact with host membranes. Resolving the mechanisms underlying these transitions remains challenging because they involve rare, cooperative motions distributed across entire viral particles. Here, we develop a full-capsid structure-based molecular dynamics framework to investigate infection-relevant dynamics in the T=3 Flock House virus capsid, a model non-enveloped RNA virus. This native contact-centric coarse-grained representation
papers.summary: This study uses a coarse-grained molecular dynamics model to simulate the entire Flock House virus capsid, revealing how a critical internal peptide becomes exposed during infection. The simulations show that the peptide externalization is a rare, cooperative event driven by specific structural rearrangements across the capsid. This work provides a mechanistic understanding of a key step in viral infection that is difficult to observe experimentally.

papers.title: Integrating machine learning and structure-based simulations to prioritise Z4P as a mutation-resilient IRE1α inhibitor for breast cancer.
papers.abstract: Breast cancer is one of the most prevalent and lethal malignancies affecting women globally. The increasing resistance to current therapeutic strategies highlights the need for novel molecular targets. Inositol-requiring enzyme 1 alpha (IRE1α), a key sensor in the unfolded protein response (UPR), has emerged as a promising therapeutic target due to its role in tumour progression and survival. This study employed an integrative in silico approach combining machine learning, molecular docking, and molecular dynamics simulations to identify potent, non-toxic IRE1α inhibitors for breast cancer tre
papers.summary: This study used machine learning and molecular simulations to identify Z4P as a potential IRE1α inhibitor for breast cancer that may be resilient to drug resistance mutations. The approach combined computational methods to prioritize compounds that are both potent and non-toxic.

papers.title: Computational and Experimental Development of a Novel Multi-Epitope Vaccine Candidate Against Bovine Leukaemia Virus.
papers.abstract: Bovine leukaemia virus (BLV) is the causative agent of enzootic bovine leukosis, a chronic infectious disease that causes significant economic losses in the dairy and beef industries worldwide. Despite extensive research, there is no licensed vaccine available for effective prevention and control of BLV infection.
papers.summary: This study uses computational methods to design a multi-epitope vaccine against Bovine Leukaemia Virus (BLV), an economically important cattle disease with no licensed vaccine. The authors identified immunogenic epitopes from BLV proteins and constructed a vaccine candidate predicted to be stable and immunogenic, though experimental validation is still needed.

papers.title: Investigation and computational prediction of gating pore currents in NaV1.2 mutations across clinical phenotypes.
papers.abstract: Voltage sensor domains (VSDs) are integral and common regions within voltage-gated ion channels that play a crucial role in cellular signaling and excitability. Each VSD comprises four transmembrane segments (S1-S4) per domain. The transmembrane segment S4 contains positively charged amino acid residues at every third position, which serve as the channel's gating charges. Over the last decade, it has been shown that these gating charges are the target of many pathological mutations. This study investigates three gating-charge mutations in domain I of the NaV1.2 channel (R220G, R223I, and R223Q
papers.summary: This study investigates how specific mutations in the voltage sensor domain of the NaV1.2 ion channel cause gating pore currents, which are linked to various clinical conditions. Using computational predictions and experimental validation, the authors show that mutations at positions R220 and R223 disrupt normal channel gating, leading to pathological leak currents. The findings provide a mechanistic understanding of how these mutations contribute to different disease severities.

papers.title: Studying and Simulating the Impact of Plasma Activated Water (PAW) on Salmonella in Almond Products: A Molecular Dynamics Investigation.
papers.abstract: Salmonella remains one of the most prevalent foodborne pathogens worldwide, significantly contributing to gastrointestinal illnesses. Therefore, it is crucial to employ methods that can effectively reduce or eliminate the presence of this harmful pathogen in agricultural products. The current study employed a continuous production system of plasma-activated water (PAW) to control and eliminate Salmonella in almonds. The study examined the impact of key operational parameters, including water flow rate, gas mixture ratio (air:argon), and the duration of applying PAW. Results demonstrated that p
papers.summary: This study investigates the use of plasma-activated water (PAW) to eliminate Salmonella contamination on almonds, finding that specific operational parameters like water flow rate and gas mixture can effectively reduce the pathogen. The research combines experimental methods with molecular dynamics simulations to understand the underlying mechanisms. This work offers a potential non-thermal decontamination approach for food safety applications.

papers.title: OrgNet+: towards robust protein stability prediction with convolutional neural networks.
papers.abstract: Predicting the effect of single-point mutations on protein stability is a central problem in molecular biology and protein engineering. Recent structure-based deep learning methods, particularly 3D convolutional neural networks (3D CNNs), have achieved strong predictive performance by leveraging high-resolution protein structures. However, proteins exist as heterogeneous conformational ensembles rather than single static structures, and the impact of conformational flexibility on structure-based ΔΔG predictors remains poorly characterized. Consequently, current models may yield unstable or eve
papers.summary: This study introduces OrgNet+, a 3D convolutional neural network that improves protein stability prediction by accounting for conformational flexibility, addressing a key limitation of existing structure-based methods that rely on single static structures. The model demonstrates more robust and stable predictions across different protein conformations, advancing the reliability of computational tools for protein engineering.

papers.title: Potential Mechanisms of Trimetazidine and Coenzyme Q10 Against Antipsychotic-Induced Myocarditis: A Network Pharmacology, Molecular Docking, and Molecular Dynamics Simulation Study.
papers.abstract: Antipsychotic-induced myocarditis is a rare but potentially fatal adverse event associated with antipsychotic treatment. Trimetazidine (TMZ) and coenzyme Q10 (CoQ10) have shown potential cardioprotective effects. Thus, they may represent adjunctive therapeutic candidates for antipsychotic-induced myocarditis. However, the underlying molecular mechanisms and therapeutic relevance of this remain unclear. This study aimed to identify the potential pharmacological mechanisms and therapeutic targets of TMZ and CoQ10 in antipsychotic-induced myocarditis.
papers.summary: This study uses computational methods to explore how trimetazidine and coenzyme Q10 might protect the heart from damage caused by antipsychotic drugs. By analyzing networks of interacting proteins and simulating how these molecules bind to targets, the authors identify potential mechanisms for their cardioprotective effects. The findings suggest these compounds could be developed as adjunctive therapies to prevent a rare but serious side effect of antipsychotic treatment.

papers.title: seq2ribo: structure-aware integration of machine learning and simulation to predict ribosome location profiles from RNA sequences.
papers.abstract: Ribosome dynamics are vital in the process of protein expression. Current methods rely on ribosome profiling (Ribo-seq), RNA-seq profiles, and full genomic context. This restricts their use in de novo sequence design, like messenger RNA (mRNA) vaccines. Simulation-only approaches like the Totally Asymmetric Simple Exclusion Process (TASEP) oversimplify translation by focusing solely on codon elongation times.
papers.summary: seq2ribo is a new computational method that combines machine learning and simulation to predict ribosome location profiles directly from RNA sequences, without needing experimental data like Ribo-seq. This enables its use in de novo sequence design, such as for mRNA vaccines, overcoming limitations of existing methods that require full genomic context. The approach improves upon simulation-only models by incorporating structure-aware features beyond just codon elongation times.

papers.title: Structure-function relationship in FGF signaling: rational design of highly stable FGF2 variants and their effect on cell growth, metabolism, and survival.
papers.abstract: Fibroblast growth factor 2 (FGF2) is a canonical member of the fibroblast growth factor family with essential roles in embryonic development, tissue regeneration, and stem cell biology, where it supports pluripotency and high proliferative capacity. Consequently, FGF2 is widely used in experimental models of regeneration and stem cell maintenance; however, its rapid thermal degradation at physiological temperatures limits its practical utility and therapeutic potential. Despite the availability of stabilized variants, developing FGF2 analogs that combine exceptional thermodynamic resilience wi
papers.summary: This study investigates how structural modifications can enhance the thermal stability of FGF2, a key growth factor used in stem cell and regeneration research. The authors designed stable FGF2 variants and tested their effects on cell growth, metabolism, and survival, aiming to overcome the protein's rapid degradation at body temperature. This work could improve the practical utility of FGF2 in experimental and therapeutic settings.

papers.title: KASSPer: Kinase Active Site Structure Prediction using Protein and Ligand Language Models and Its Application to Virtual Screening.
papers.abstract: Structure-based virtual screening (SBVS) is limited by the rigid-receptor assumption, which is particularly problematic for kinases that adopt multiple active-site conformations but are experimentally biased toward a single state. Although ensemble screening can address this limitation, it remains computationally expensive.
papers.summary: KASSPer is a new computational method that uses protein and ligand language models to predict kinase active site structures, enabling more accurate virtual screening by accounting for the multiple conformations kinases can adopt. This approach overcomes the limitations of traditional rigid-receptor virtual screening without the high computational cost of ensemble methods.

papers.title: In Silico Drug Repositioning Identifies SYK Kinase Inhibitors as Potential Neuroprotective Agents for Ataxia-Telangiectasia.
papers.abstract: Ataxia-Telangiectasia (AT) is a rare neurodegenerative disorder characterized by progressive neuronal loss and chronic neuroinflammation. Emerging evidence indicates that aberrant overexpression of the adaptor protein TYROBP promotes sustained recruitment and activation of spleen tyrosine kinase (SYK), contributing to pathogenic inflammatory signaling in AT. In this study, we applied a drug repositioning strategy to identify clinically approved compounds that inhibit SYK activity. An integrated in silico workflow was employed, combining convolutional neural network (CNN)-based molecular dockin
papers.summary: This study uses computational drug repositioning to identify FDA-approved SYK kinase inhibitors as potential treatments for Ataxia-Telangiectasia, a rare neurodegenerative disease. The authors developed an in silico pipeline combining deep learning-based molecular docking to screen for compounds that could block the aberrant SYK signaling linked to neuroinflammation in this disorder.

papers.title: Preventing physical and chemical degradation of the LABL-Fc-MOGR5, a bifunctional peptide inhibitor, with formulation development approaches.
papers.abstract: LABL-Fc-MOGR5 is a bifunctional peptide inhibitor designed to modulate immune responses in multiple sclerosis, including primary progressive multiple sclerosis. Although therapeutically promising, its development has been hindered by poor physical stability, particularly rapid precipitation under standard formulation conditions.
papers.summary: This study identifies and addresses the physical instability of a bifunctional peptide inhibitor for multiple sclerosis, showing that formulation approaches can prevent its rapid precipitation. The work provides a path forward for developing this therapeutic candidate.

papers.title: Uncovering antioxidant and anti-breast cancer potential metabolite from streptomyces strain sp. VITAMB using integrated experimental and computational approaches.
papers.abstract: Actinobacteria's are rich in bioactive compounds, showing antimicrobial, antiviral, antioxidative, anti-inflammatory, and anticancer activity. The increasing demand for these metabolites in the culinary, pharmaceutical, cosmetic, and nutraceutical industries emphasizes the latent potential of actinomycetes. Paddy field ecosystem provides unique physicochemical conditions that favor the growth of actinomycetes, making them a valuable reservoir of pigment-producing microorganisms. In the present study, a yellow pigment-producing strain identified as Streptomyces sp (GenBank ID: OR958670) was iso
papers.summary: This study identifies a yellow pigment-producing Streptomyces strain from paddy soil with antioxidant and anti-breast cancer activity, using both lab experiments and computational modeling. The findings suggest this bacterial metabolite could be a promising candidate for developing natural antioxidant and anticancer therapies.

papers.title: Experimental and Mechanistic Validation of PARP1pred for Identifying Potent Leads.
papers.abstract: Poly(adenosine diphosphate-ribose) polymerase 1 (PARP1) is a pivotal target for treating homologous recombination-deficient cancers through the mechanism of synthetic lethality. While machine learning has accelerated the identification of novel inhibitors, many models lack experimental validation and high-resolution mechanistic insights. In this study, we evaluated the predictive robustness of the PARP1pred model using a hierarchical pipeline. Initial bioactivity predictions for candidates in unseen chemical space were validated through biochemical and cellular sensitivity assays using a panel
papers.summary: This study validates the PARP1pred machine learning model for identifying new PARP1 inhibitors, which are important for treating certain cancers. The model's predictions were confirmed through biochemical and cellular experiments, showing it can find effective drug leads in new chemical space.

papers.title: Identification and experimental validation of CD74, PGLYRP1, and TXN as potential biomarkers in rheumatoid arthritis: an integrative bulk and ScRNA-seq study.
papers.abstract: Chronic joint inflammation, the hallmark of rheumatoid arthritis (RA), is an autoimmune condition that commonly leads to progressive joint damage and dysfunction. While several clinical biomarkers are available for diagnosing and predicting RA, their specificity and sensitivity are still insufficient. Therefore, the objective was to discover biomarkers associated with RA and delineate their functional mechanisms.
papers.summary: This study identified CD74, PGLYRP1, and TXN as potential biomarkers for rheumatoid arthritis by integrating bulk and single-cell RNA sequencing data, and experimentally validated their expression. These biomarkers may improve diagnostic specificity and sensitivity for RA, and the findings suggest they play roles in immune cell infiltration and inflammatory pathways.

papers.title: Integrated multi-omics analysis reveals distinct microbiota-metabolite signatures and a novel HCN2-2-hydroxybutyric acid interaction in inflammatory bowel disease.
papers.abstract: Gut microbiota-derived short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) exert critical regulatory functions in inflammatory bowel disease (IBD). However, integrated profiling of fecal SCFA signatures alongside gut microbiota composition in ulcerative colitis (UC) and Crohn's disease (CD) remains insufficiently characterized. Furthermore, the molecular mechanisms through which microbiota metabolites engage host protein targets warrant systematic investigation.
papers.summary: This study profiles gut microbiota and short-chain fatty acids in ulcerative colitis and Crohn's disease, identifying distinct metabolite signatures for each subtype. It also uncovers a novel interaction between the ion channel HCN2 and 2-hydroxybutyric acid, suggesting a potential molecular mechanism linking microbial metabolites to host targets in inflammatory bowel disease.

papers.title: Liposomal Nanoparticulate Drug Delivery Systems: Strategies to Destabilize Biological Membranes at the Target Tissue.
papers.abstract: Liposomal nanoparticulate drug delivery systems (LNDDSs) are clinically validated nanomedicine platforms seeing regular use in oncology and infectious disease. Their applications have rapidly expanded with several tissue targeting formulations in early-phase clinical trials. Beyond small molecular drugs, LNDDSs are increasingly employed for delivery of nucleic acid therapeutics, such as ribonucleic acid (RNA) based vaccines and immunomodulators. Recent advances in nanomaterials have enabled LNDDSs not only to transport therapeutic agents across systemic biological barriers but also to selectiv
papers.summary: This review discusses liposomal nanoparticulate drug delivery systems (LNDDSs), which are clinically used nanomedicines for oncology and infectious diseases, and are now expanding to deliver nucleic acid therapeutics like RNA vaccines. It focuses on strategies to destabilize biological membranes at target tissues to improve selective delivery of therapeutic agents.

papers.title: Design, Synthesis and Biological Evaluation of New Oxazolidinone Antimicrobial Agents with Low Monoamine Oxidase A Inhibition.
papers.abstract: Linezolid is an oxazolidinone antibacterial agent that acts by binding to the 23S rRNA of the bacterial 50S ribosomal subunit to inhibit protein synthesis and exhibits potent activity against Gram-positive pathogens and Mycobacterium tuberculosis. Although resistance to linezolid remains relatively low, its clinical use is limited by safety concerns arising from inhibition of monoamine oxidase A (MAO-A). To address this limitation, we designed and synthesized a series of novel oxazolidinone derivatives with reduced MAO-A inhibition and preserved antibacterial potency.
papers.summary: Researchers designed new oxazolidinone antibiotics that retain antibacterial activity against Gram-positive bacteria and Mycobacterium tuberculosis while reducing the dangerous side effect of monoamine oxidase A inhibition seen with the existing drug linezolid. This work aims to improve the safety profile of this important antibiotic class.

papers.title: Photo-responsive nanozymes: from photocatalytic mechanisms to precision therapy.
papers.abstract: Conventional nanozymes still suffer from limited catalytic efficiency and poor substrate selectivity, which impede their clinical translation. Recently, photo-responsive nanozymes have emerged as a promising platform for precision biomedicine by integrating photocatalytic processes with enzyme-mimetic catalysis, enabling spatiotemporally controlled enhancement of catalytic activity. In this review, we highlight strategies for incorporating emerging photocatalytic technologies into the rational design of nanozymes and systematically elucidate the core mechanisms by which photogenerated charge c
papers.summary: This review discusses how combining photocatalytic processes with enzyme-mimetic catalysis can create 'photo-responsive nanozymes' that overcome the limited efficiency and selectivity of conventional nanozymes. These hybrid materials enable spatiotemporally controlled catalytic activity for precision biomedical applications. The paper systematically outlines design strategies and core mechanisms involving photogenerated charge carriers.
